title: Databases Were Not Designed For This
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-27, Reading time: 16 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Farpitbhayani.me%2Fblogs%2Fdefensive-databases%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dcea2ec09-68a45034-73e1-4046-9983-6599dc6910af-000000/hHjKkJbRY9TIZpdA2qIyhcxAYVl3wSrpFA3NfNai0xY=452

summary: Revisiting the Outcome Distortion Complex (5 minute read) Uber burned cash, courted controversy, and became a massive public company. WeWork did the same things and went bankrupt. The behavior didn't change, only the verdict did. In 2022, investors admitted ZIRP-era excess had pushed companies to build for the wrong environment. The same dynamics are back now under a cleaner label called AI, and "this time we know what we're doing" has historically not aged well.

summary: Agent Memory Patterns (7 minute read) This article discusses how to add memory to agents. The three most common types of mutable memory are Files, Memory Blocks, and Skills. Files are for data and knowledge, Memory Blocks are a flat key-value store, and Skills are indexed files that contain data or instructions that are only needed in certain circumstances. Editable memory makes agents extremely powerful.

title: OpenSSH Flaw Allowing Full Root Shell Access Lurked for 15 Years
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 2 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2Fj2Qv4o/1/0100019dd4358207-84f7e3c0-d39a-4a57-a42b-1efb325a77d8-000000/eF8l2ww8REISU4r-BRaC2i8Z-3B60Ue_uwZ5iHmdDV4=452
summary: OpenSSH Flaw Allowing Full Root Shell Access Lurked for 15 Years (2 minute read) CVE-2026-35414 affects OpenSSH versions from the past 15 years. A code reuse error allows commas in SSH certificate principals to be parsed as list separators. If a certificate contains "deploy,root" as a principal, OpenSSH splits on the comma and grants root access. The attack leaves no authentication failure in logs and researchers created a working exploit in twenty minutes. OpenSSH 10.3 patches the flaw.

title: Batch API is terrible for one agent. It might be great for a fleet
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Feran.sandler.co.il%2Fpost%2F2026-04-27-batch-api-is-terrible-for-one-agent%2F%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd43fd7ba-2b251623-4fe1-44a4-99ee-bf2819091b58-000000/iTYNNlzxSkOELiaJ9uBU5hcJpfpSBx88MUsENbtYaSw=452
summary: Batch API is terrible for one agent. It might be great for a fleet (6 minute read) Batch API offers a 50% discount but adds latency, making it less suitable for single-agent use. For fleets of agents where multiple requests can be pooled, the batching approach becomes economically viable. Optimal usage involves routing slower, costlier models through batches, while employing faster models via synchronous paths, potentially managed by intelligent proxies like the developing LunaRoute.

title: GPT 5.5: The System Card

summary: GPT 5.5: The System Card (20 minute read) GPT-5.5 is a solid improvement and is competitive with Claude Opus. It seems to be better for factual queries, web searches, and straightforward, well-specified requests, while Claude Opus excels in more open-ended or interpretive purposes. The model is unlikely to pose new big risks, and its alignment seems similar to that of previous models. This post examines the system card for GPT-5.5.

title: Compressing AI vectors to 2–4 bits per number without losing accuracy
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 54 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Farkaung.github.io%2Finteractive-turboquant%2F%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd43fd7ba-2b251623-4fe1-44a4-99ee-bf2819091b58-000000/neruG57lo0lql8zLEHPoF_tGDTwhCoKB1GSoI4O205A=452
summary: Compressing AI vectors to 2–4 bits per number without losing accuracy (54 minute read) TurboQuant compresses each coordinate in large tables of high-dimensional vectors to 2-4 bits with provably near-optimal distortion, no memory overhead for scale factors, and no training or calibration. It is between four and six orders of magnitude faster than the alternatives at 4-bit indexing, with higher recall as well. This page explains how TurboQuant works.

summary: Recursive Language Models, clearly explained (3 minute read) MIT researchers have introduced Recursive Language Models (RLMs) to solve "context rot," a phenomenon where large language models experience reasoning degradation when processing massive context windows, even if they excel at basic retrieval tasks. title: The Moat or the Commons
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 10 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FTGeuCw/1/0100019dd43fd7ba-2b251623-4fe1-44a4-99ee-bf2819091b58-000000/IbK_lDJQhsSSM27Ke0R5Zg7eCzdJqSDKS9Ge1pJKArc=452
summary: The Moat or the Commons (10 minute read) American AI was financed on the bet that frontier models would be the next great monopoly business. That assumption is now breaking as open weight models are commoditizing the capability that the American capital paid-for moat was supposed to protect. The gap between the open frontier and the closed frontier is closing. The question will be whether countries choose to subsidize the private moat or the open commons.

title: Software Is Eating the World
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 32 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FwY7qCz/1/0100019dd8c35b9f-2c63426e-5c12-4b37-858e-f95e3e252335-000000/IeEEf0tvhuwLmuErN8IWl4o5YlFbTC-hdX8qBWvvPKc=452
summary: Software Is Eating the World (But Actually This Time) (32 minute read) Software ate distribution, but most of the work was still done by humans. AI changes that - the work is now becoming software. Agents can read, reason, call tools, verify, revise, and perform long-running tasks. As models commoditize, the apps that capture messy operational data will be the ones to improve fastest and defend their position longest.

summary: We Upgraded to a Frontier Model and Our Costs Went Down (5 minute read) Mendral reduced costs by using a multi-agent architecture where a cheap "Haiku triager" handles 80% of routine CI log failures, escalating only complex issues to a "frontier" Opus model. This tiered system allows the expensive model to focus on orchestration and delegation while low-cost agents perform the data-heavy tasks, resulting in significantly more efficient LLM operations.

title: Why Your AI Agent Is Drowning in Tools
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fengineering.leanix.net%2Fblog%2Fcode-mode%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dd8ef31da-b6a88cf5-5eba-4385-a788-1a59edb57284-000000/TuTLM78dqGXxT44F71jOciFCYnJnioKCCx8b8sEfR34=452
summary: Why Your AI Agent Is Drowning in Tools (And How Code Mode Saves It) (5 minute read) Too many MCP tools bloat context and cause hallucinations. Two fixes include reducing tools (filter agent-side or design fewer use-case-driven ones MCP-side), or use “code mode.” This means give the LLM the ability to search and execute, let it write code against a generated SDK, and run multi-step workflows in a sandbox.

summary: Who Owns the Code Claude Wrote? summary: A New Token Rule For Engineering Leadership (7 minute read) Chainguard now expects its engineering managers to be at the 50th percentile of token usage among their direct reports. title: The Brave New World of AI Markets
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FYsSYj6/1/0100019dd9249a78-66c9acfa-e59d-4d83-b1f6-96ceb00feaac-000000/hX5xz0kwTaqPrGdCHxMPC6woNe-oJXrlXI2G9WCyn6U=452
summary: The Brave New World of AI Markets (5 minute read) It is naive to assume that the Total Addressable Market (TAM) for artificial intelligence will simply equal the current size of the global human labor market. This post proposes a new framework for sizing AI markets based on the classic economic equation of Price multiplied by Quantity (TAM = Human Labor TAM × Price Compression Factor × Volume Expansion Factor).

title: Direction Over Speed
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 7 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.leahtharin.com%2Fp%2Fdirection-over-speed%3Futm_source=tldrfounders/1/0100019dd9249a78-66c9acfa-e59d-4d83-b1f6-96ceb00feaac-000000/fZ_IyO1JoF5OonKq-_SMvQUjeWOBCPvbZMgBuW202gU=452
summary: Direction Over Speed (7 minute read) For most of software's history, you could fake productivity by shipping a lot. AI broke that cover. Prototypes are cheap, specs write themselves, and shipping looks the same whether you're building the right thing or the wrong one. Smaller teams of four or five, with the PM closer to the work, especially on AI features where "is this good" can't be answered by a metric. The model can build the thing for you. It still can't tell you why anyone should care.

title: Shipped Isn't Solved
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwildfirelabs.substack.com%2Fp%2Fshipped-isnt-solved%3Futm_source=tldrfounders/1/0100019dd9249a78-66c9acfa-e59d-4d83-b1f6-96ceb00feaac-000000/LuFy5jxDr_eLyFDrrtcUtzqeEaAbty-sZAJRDkbt2VA=452
summary: Shipped Isn't Solved (5 minute read) A customer asked for a feature on a call. The team built it, shipped it fast, and moved on. Nobody really used it. title: Agent Auth: Why OAuth Wasn't Built for This
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.apideck.com%2Fblog%2Fagent-auth-oauth-ai-agents%23where-this-leaves-builders%3Futm_source=tldrit/1/0100019dd9285b20-7650f0b9-32fb-4b19-b5b5-ab86dded81ca-000000/nsy3WH5oOactSjd6-uhxMwW9_nUuPczeql8R5o0aJEM=452
summary: Agent Auth: Why OAuth Wasn't Built for This (11 minute read) Standard OAuth 2.0 fails to support AI agents that make runtime decisions and delegate tasks. title: Output isn't design
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinear.app%2Fnow%2Foutput-isn-t-design%3Futm_source=tldrdesign/1/0100019dd934fd98-551bb87b-a592-44e7-9728-f3d36824a554-000000/qnas5Y8_Ecjj7_LfzbF0Pr64KffhrHmyLOzJz2pz_pw=452
summary: Output isn't design (4 minute read) Design isn't about producing outputs but about deeply understanding a problem and achieving a good fit between form and context. summary: Open source package with 1 million monthly downloads stole user credentials (3 minute read) Attackers abused a GitHub Actions flaw in the element-data project to steal signing keys, then shipped a malicious 0.23.3 release that exfiltrated warehouse credentials, cloud keys, API tokens, SSH keys, and env contents before it was removed about 12 hours later. Affected users should install 0.23.4, check for the trinny marker file, purge caches, and rotate exposed secrets.

title: Can agents replace the search stack?
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 7 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fsoftwaredoug.com%2Fblog%2F2026%2F04%2F28%2Fsearch-apis-replaced-by-agents.html%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd9660561-7494fa10-ad37-4470-ba39-c2014b84ad06-000000/ijzh200dDuPnypmDGYrl8vkqDxOnUqU7pB1GsLE041o=452
summary: Can agents replace the search stack? (7 minute read) Giving agents basic search tools results in better quality answers. Encouraging the agent to explore further creates more improvements. Agentic search models can figure out how to surface relevant results, but the training data still dictates the models' sweet spots. Large language models can't evaluate what they don't know, and there's still no real way to compensate for their lack of knowledge.

title: Opus 4.7's New Tokenizer: What It Actually Costs
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fopenrouter.ai%2Fannouncements%2Fopus-47-tokenizer-analysis%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd9660561-7494fa10-ad37-4470-ba39-c2014b84ad06-000000/cflYbd2NyySjmsNXIp-Ehro-1vRczbnfUNa62fJj_rw=452
summary: Opus 4.7's New Tokenizer: What It Actually Costs (6 minute read) Anthropic improved Claude Opus 4.7's understanding of inputs with a new tokenizer. Costs have increased between 12% to 27%, except for short prompts, which have actually become more cost-efficient.

summary: OpenAI's Q4 2026 IPO Might not Happen (9 minute read) OpenAI's CFO allegedly doesn't report to the CEO and reportedly has been excluded from financial discussions involving server procurement. The company has committed to enormous infrastructure obligations, but the CFO and board are unsure whether those commitments can be funded. These factors, along with the compressed IPO timeline, make it unlikely that OpenAI will be able to IPO this year.

title: The Trust Problem With AI Agents
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: Sponsor, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Ftldr.tech%2Fblog%2Ftrust-problem-ai-agents%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd9660561-7494fa10-ad37-4470-ba39-c2014b84ad06-000000/fn05K2eOsnhWtoQO4XrbiRspbrymU_Zkvg4h_CaFyJU=452
summary: The Trust Problem With AI Agents (Sponsor) AI agents are getting more capable, but developers are trusting them less. The problem isn't the technology, but the design of the tools they're using. Here's why human-in-the-loop actually matters, and what it should look like. Join the waitlist and follow the 5-part series.

title: Laguna XS.2 and M.1: A Deeper Dive
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 20 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fpoolside.ai%2Fblog%2Flaguna-a-deeper-dive%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd9660561-7494fa10-ad37-4470-ba39-c2014b84ad06-000000/iGDzMI09fHLwMlNJtTEBHMUTrZ-58-ngCYwzHt-fhSM=452
summary: Laguna XS.2 and M.1: A Deeper Dive (20 minute read) Laguna XS.2 and Laguna M.1 are agentic coding models built for long-horizon work. Laguna M.1 is the foundation for the Laguna family of models. Laguna XS.2 is a much smaller model but is still remarkably capable for its size. Both models are free to use for a limited time via Poolside's API and on OpenRouter. The weights for Laguna XS.2 have been released under an Apache 2.0 license.

summary: The Recurrent Transformer: Greater Effective Depth and Efficient Decoding (5 minute read) The Recurrent Transformer is a novel architecture designed to overcome the "temporal shallowness" of standard Transformers. By introducing layerwise recurrence, the model uses a combination of temporary and persistent key-value pairs to allow each layer to maintain a continuously updating internal memory.
